(**NY 17**) is a major [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") that extends for 397 miles (638.91 km) through the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") and [Downstate](/wiki/Downstate_New_York "Downstate New York") regions of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It begins at the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in [Mina](/wiki/Mina,_New_York "Mina, New York") and runs east as a [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway"), following the **Southern Tier Expressway** to [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton "Binghamton") and the **Quickway** from Binghamton east to [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury,_Orange_County,_New_York "Woodbury, Orange County, New York"). At Woodbury, the route turns south to become a [local arterial](/wiki/Arterial_road "Arterial road"), eventually following the **Orange Turnpike** to the [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") state line near [Suffern](/wiki/Suffern,_New_York "Suffern, New York"). The portion of NY 17 west of Woodbury is in the process of being upgraded to [Interstate 86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)"), and as of February 2025, the routes are [concurrent](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") from the Pennsylvania border to [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York") and from [Kirkwood](/wiki/Kirkwood,_New_York "Kirkwood, New York") to [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York").

**New York State Route 17** (**NY 17**) is a major [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") that extends for 397 miles (638.91 km) through the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") and [Downstate](/wiki/Downstate_New_York "Downstate New York") regions of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It begins at the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in [Mina](/wiki/Mina,_New_York "Mina, New York") and runs east as a [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway"), following the **Southern Tier Expressway** to [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton "Binghamton") and the **Quickway** from Binghamton east to [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury,_Orange_County,_New_York "Woodbury, Orange County, New York"). At Woodbury, the route turns south to become a [local arterial](/wiki/Arterial_road "Arterial road"), eventually following the **Orange Turnpike** to the [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") state line near [Suffern](/wiki/Suffern,_New_York "Suffern, New York"). The portion of NY 17 west of Woodbury is in the process of being upgraded to [Interstate 86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)"), and as of February 2025, the routes are [concurrent](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") from the Pennsylvania border to [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York") and from [Kirkwood](/wiki/Kirkwood,_New_York "Kirkwood, New York") to [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York").

At 397 miles (639 km), NY 17 is the longest state route in New York, and is the second-longest highway of any kind in the state, behind only the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway"). It serves 11 counties, passes through the cities of [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York"), [Olean](/wiki/Olean_(city),_New_York "Olean (city), New York"), [Corning](/wiki/Corning,_New_York "Corning, New York"), [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York"), and Binghamton, and enters the vicinity of several others, including [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") and [Middletown](/wiki/Middletown,_Orange_County,_New_York "Middletown, Orange County, New York"). As it proceeds across the state, it intersects many of New York's major [Interstate](/wiki/Interstate_Highway "Interstate Highway") and [U.S. Highways](/wiki/U.S._Highway "U.S. Highway"), including [U.S. Route 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219_in_New_York "U.S. Route 219 in New York") (US 219) in Salamanca, [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") in [Avoca](/wiki/Avoca,_New_York "Avoca, New York"), [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99_in_New_York "Interstate 99 in New York") and [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York") near Corning, [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") and [US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11_in_New_York "U.S. Route 11 in New York") in Binghamton, [I-84](/wiki/Interstate_84_in_New_York "Interstate 84 in New York") and [US 6](/wiki/U.S._Route_6_in_New_York "U.S. Route 6 in New York") near Middletown, and [I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") in Woodbury. Aside from a brief concurrency with the Thruway near [Hillburn](/wiki/Hillburn,_New_York "Hillburn, New York"), the route is maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation"), including a portion in the vicinity of [Waverly](/wiki/Waverly_(village),_New_York "Waverly (village), New York") that is physically located in Pennsylvania.

Initially part of an [auto trail](/wiki/Auto_trail "Auto trail") called the [Liberty Highway](/wiki/Liberty_Highway "Liberty Highway"), the route was added to the state highway system in 1924, extending from [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield_(village),_New_York "Westfield (village), New York") to Suffern via at-grade highways. As traffic demand surged, it was moved onto the Quickway and the Southern Tier Expressway as limited-access sections of both were completed from the 1950s to the 1990s. In 1998, all of NY 17 between the Pennsylvania state line and Woodbury was designated as "Future I-86". The westernmost 177 miles (285 km) of the route was designated as I-86 one year later, and the designation has been gradually extended eastward as sections of NY 17 were improved to Interstate Highway standards. Prior to the I-86 designation, NY 17 was part of a multi-state Route 17 along with [New Jersey Route 17](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_17 "New Jersey Route 17") and the former Pennsylvania Route 17.

### Pennsylvania to Elmira

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pennsylvania to Elmira")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg/120px-Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg)

Route marker used along the Southern Tier Expressway

Main article: [Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)")

NY 17 begins as a [freeway](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") at the point where I-86 crosses the [New York–Pennsylvania border](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in [Mina](/wiki/Mina,_New_York "Mina, New York"), [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"). I-86 heads westward from there to its western terminus at [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 90 in Pennsylvania"). I-86 and NY 17 continue eastward through the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"), encountering [NY 426](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_426 "New York State Route 426") (exit 4) a short distance from the state line prior to meeting [NY 76](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_76 "New York State Route 76") (exit 6) south of [Sherman](/wiki/Sherman_(village),_New_York "Sherman (village), New York"). East of exit 8 ([NY 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394")), I-86 and NY 17 cross [Chautauqua Lake](/wiki/Chautauqua_Lake "Chautauqua Lake") and follow the lake shore eastward to [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), where it connects to [NY 60](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_60 "New York State Route 60") at exit 12 due north of the city. East of the city, the freeway meets [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_New_York "U.S. Route 62 in New York") at exit 14 and is joined by the old [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") line, which parallels the freeway as it heads across southern New York.

Between exits 17 and 18 ([NY 280](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_280 "New York State Route 280")), I-86 and NY 17 cross the [Allegheny Reservoir](/wiki/Allegheny_Reservoir "Allegheny Reservoir") near its northernmost extent. Past NY 280, the freeway runs adjacent to the northern extent of the [Allegany State Park](/wiki/Allegany_State_Park "Allegany State Park") and follows the reservoir and the connecting [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") eastward to [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York"). Near downtown Salamanca, I-86 and NY 17 meet [US 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219_in_New_York "U.S. Route 219 in New York") (exit 21). US 219 joins the freeway east to exit 23 near [Carrollton](/wiki/Carrollton,_New_York "Carrollton, New York"), where it splits from I-86 and NY 17 and heads toward [Bradford, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Bradford,_Pennsylvania "Bradford, Pennsylvania"), forming the eastern edge of the state park as it heads south. Meanwhile, the freeway continues east to [Olean](/wiki/Olean,_New_York "Olean, New York"), where it meets [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") (a previous alignment of NY 17) at exit 24 west of town and [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") (exit 27) north of the area.

Past Olean, the route drifts northward away from Pennsylvania toward [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York"), where I-86 and NY 17 intersect [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") (exit 34). To the east in [Avoca](/wiki/Avoca_(village),_New_York "Avoca (village), New York"), the Southern Tier Expressway meets [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") at exit 36. I-86 and NY 17 southeast from the junction, passing through [Bath](/wiki/Bath_(village),_New_York "Bath (village), New York") on its way an interchange with [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99") and [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York") in [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York") (exit 44). Here, I-99 and US 15 begin and head south toward Pennsylvania, while I-86 and NY 17 continue east through [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York") to the city of [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York").

### Elmira to Harriman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Elmira to Harriman")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/State_border_sign_on_NY_17.jpg/250px-State_border_sign_on_NY_17.jpg)](/wiki/File:State_border_sign_on_NY_17.jpg)

Sign along eastbound NY 17 marking return to New York after its brief foray into Pennsylvania

From Elmira to Binghamton, NY 17, the Erie Railroad (now operated by [Norfolk Southern](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern "Norfolk Southern") as the [Southern Tier Line](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Line "Southern Tier Line")), and its old alignments generally stay close together. They follow the Chemung River to exit 60 ([US 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220 "U.S. Route 220") in [South Waverly, Pennsylvania](/wiki/South_Waverly,_Pennsylvania "South Waverly, Pennsylvania")) and the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") from east of exit 61 ([Waverly, New York](/wiki/Waverly,_Tioga_County,_New_York "Waverly, Tioga County, New York")) to Binghamton; on the latter section, both [NY 17C](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17C "New York State Route 17C") and [NY 434](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_434 "New York State Route 434") are old NY 17. Between the two rivers, which intersect in Pennsylvania, the general corridor runs just north of the state line in New York. NY 17 itself crosses into Pennsylvania for approximately 1 mile (1.6 km) between a point west of exit 60 and a point west of exit 61; additionally, all the ramps at exit 60 and portions of the eastbound ramps at exits 59A and 61 are in Pennsylvania. Despite being in Pennsylvania, it is still signed as NY 17, and these roadways are still maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) At the Tioga County line near Waverly, I-86 temporarily terminates as NY 17 continues eastward toward [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton_(city),_New_York "Binghamton (city), New York").

Near downtown Binghamton, NY 17 goes around the side of Prospect Mountain at what is locally known as "[kamikaze](/wiki/Kamikaze "Kamikaze") curve".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Heading eastbound, the freeway curves sharply left around the hillside, splits into ramps to [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") north and south, and curves right to merge into I-81 south as it passes over the [Chenango River](/wiki/Chenango_River "Chenango River"). From that point east and southeast about 5 miles (8.0 km), I-81 and NY 17 run [concurrently](/wiki/Concurrent_(road) "Concurrent (road)"). NY 17 splits from I-81, the Erie Railroad and the Susquehanna River to the east into [Stilson Hollow](/w/index.php?title=Stilson_Hollow&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stilson Hollow (page does not exist)"); from this split (exit 75) to its end, most of NY 17 does not follow the Erie Railroad, which crosses into Pennsylvania several times.

At the end of Stilson Hollow, NY 17 heads over a summit and into the valley formed by the [Occanum Creek](/w/index.php?title=Occanum_Creek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Occanum Creek (page does not exist)"). The creek empties into the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") at [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York") (exit 79), which NY 17 follows southeast to [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus,_New_York "Damascus, New York") (exit 80) before turning northeast along [Tuscarora Creek](/w/index.php?title=Tuscarora_Creek_(New_York)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tuscarora Creek (New York) (page does not exist)"). It soon turns east and southeast over a summit, rejoining the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") just north of [Gulf Summit](/wiki/Gulf_Summit,_New_York "Gulf Summit, New York"). The highway and railroad head east along [Oquaga Creek](/wiki/Oquaga_Creek "Oquaga Creek") to [Deposit](/wiki/Deposit_(village),_New_York "Deposit (village), New York") (exit 84), where they turn southeast along the [West Branch Delaware River](/wiki/West_Branch_Delaware_River "West Branch Delaware River"), where NY 17 briefly becomes an arterial road. A gap in the freeway stretches from here to just short of [Hancock](/wiki/Hancock_(village),_New_York "Hancock (village), New York") (exit 87), the place the West Branch joins with the [East Branch Delaware River](/wiki/East_Branch_Delaware_River "East Branch Delaware River"). The Erie Railroad continues southeast along the combined [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"), while NY 17 turns east along the valley formed by the East Branch, either closely following or built directly over the abandoned [New York, Ontario and Western Railway](/wiki/New_York,_Ontario_and_Western_Railway "New York, Ontario and Western Railway")[[6]](#cite_note-6) to [Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_(village),_New_York "Liberty (village), New York").

At [East Branch](/wiki/East_Branch,_New_York "East Branch, New York") (exit 90), the East Branch Delaware River turns north, and NY 17 continues east with the [Beaver Kill](/wiki/Beaver_Kill "Beaver Kill") to [Roscoe](/wiki/Roscoe,_New_York "Roscoe, New York") (exit 94), [Willowemoc Creek](/wiki/Willowemoc_Creek "Willowemoc Creek") to [Livingston Manor](/wiki/Livingston_Manor,_New_York "Livingston Manor, New York") (exit 96), and [Little Beaver Kill](/w/index.php?title=Little_Beaver_Kill&action=edit&redlink=1 "Little Beaver Kill (page does not exist)") to [Parksville](/wiki/Parksville,_New_York "Parksville, New York") (exit 98). The highway and parallel NYO&W pass south over a summit to [Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_(village),_New_York "Liberty (village), New York") (exits 99–100), and continue along the [Middle Mongaup River](/w/index.php?title=Middle_Mongaup_River&action=edit&redlink=1 "Middle Mongaup River (page does not exist)") to [Ferndale](/wiki/Ferndale,_New_York "Ferndale, New York") (exit 101). The NYO&W turned east there, but NY 17 continues south over a summit and into the [Spring Brook](/w/index.php?title=Spring_Brook_(New_York)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Spring Brook (New York) (page does not exist)") and [East Mongaup River](/w/index.php?title=East_Mongaup_River&action=edit&redlink=1 "East Mongaup River (page does not exist)") valleys past [Harris](/wiki/Harris,_New_York "Harris, New York") (exit 102). NY 17 then cuts southeast cross-country to [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello,_New_York "Monticello, New York") (exit 104; passing [Monticello Raceway](/wiki/Monticello_Raceway "Monticello Raceway")) and beyond, following the old [Newburgh and Cochecton Turnpike](/wiki/Newburgh_and_Cochecton_Turnpike "Newburgh and Cochecton Turnpike") (old NY 17) to [Bloomingburg](/wiki/Bloomingburg,_New_York "Bloomingburg, New York") (exit 116). The old [Middletown and Wurtsboro Turnpike](/w/index.php?title=Middletown_and_Wurtsboro_Turnpike&action=edit&redlink=1 "Middletown and Wurtsboro Turnpike (page does not exist)"), also old NY 17, and partially [NY 17M](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17M "New York State Route 17M"), runs south to [Middletown](/wiki/Middletown,_Orange_County,_New_York "Middletown, Orange County, New York"), which NY 17 cuts cross-country to bypass to the east, rejoining NY 17M – and the main line of the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") – at [Goshen](/wiki/Goshen_(village),_New_York "Goshen (village), New York") (exit 123). NY 17, its old former alignment (NY 17M) and the Erie run generally east-southeast, partly cross-country and partly through small stream valleys, to the end of the freeway, the directional change in NY 17 from east–west to north–south, and the junction of the Erie with its branch to [Newburgh](/wiki/Newburgh,_New_York "Newburgh, New York").

### Harriman to New Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Harriman to New Jersey")]

NY 17 heads southwest from the Quickway as a surface road, passing through the village of [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury,_Orange_County,_New_York "Woodbury, Orange County, New York") before entering the village of [Harriman](/wiki/Harriman,_New_York "Harriman, New York"), where it intersects with the eastern terminus of NY 17M.[[7]](#cite_note-HarrimanNJ-7) As the route heads southward from this junction, its signage changes from being east or west to north or south. The route parallels the Thruway as it proceeds through a disjointed piece of [Harriman State Park](/wiki/Harriman_State_Park_(New_York) "Harriman State Park (New York)") and enters the town of [Tuxedo](/wiki/Tuxedo,_New_York "Tuxedo, New York"). While inside the park, NY 17 intersects [Arden Valley Road](/wiki/Arden_Valley_Road "Arden Valley Road"), a parkway that connects to [Seven Lakes Drive](/wiki/Seven_Lakes_Drive "Seven Lakes Drive") deep inside the park.[[8]](#cite_note-map4-8) South of Arden Valley Road, NY 17 briefly exits Harriman State Park and enters the hamlet of [Southfields](/wiki/Southfields,_New_York "Southfields, New York"), where it intersects with [County Route 19](/wiki/County_Route_19_(Orange_County,_New_York) "County Route 19 (Orange County, New York)") (CR 19) and passes by the [Red Apple Rest](/wiki/Red_Apple_Rest "Red Apple Rest"), a former restaurant and roadside attraction.[[9]](#cite_note-map3-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/2017-09-10_10_01_54_View_north_along_Interstate_287_and_New_York_State_Route_17_%28Middlesex_Freeway%29_just_south_of_Interstate_87_%28New_York_State_Thruway%29_within_Hillburn_Village_in_the_Town_of_Ramapo%2C_Rockland_County%2C_New_York.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:2017-09-10_10_01_54_View_north_along_Interstate_287_and_New_York_State_Route_17_(Middlesex_Freeway)_just_south_of_Interstate_87_(New_York_State_Thruway)_within_Hillburn_Village_in_the_Town_of_Ramapo,_Rockland_County,_New_York.jpg)

View north along NY 17 and I-287 as it enters New York from New Jersey

Past the hamlet, the route heads back into the park and intersects with [NY 17A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17A "New York State Route 17A") and [CR 106](/wiki/County_Route_106_(Orange_County,_New_York) "County Route 106 (Orange County, New York)") near the park's western boundary. South of the junction, NY 17 leaves the park and proceeds through a lightly populated area to the village of [Tuxedo Park](/wiki/Tuxedo_Park,_New_York "Tuxedo Park, New York"). At the Tuxedo Park train station is access to some hiking trails in Harriman State Park.[[9]](#cite_note-map3-9) The route continues on, paralleling the Thruway into [Rockland County](/wiki/Rockland_County,_New_York "Rockland County, New York"). On the other side of the county line, NY 17 enters the village of [Sloatsburg](/wiki/Sloatsburg,_New_York "Sloatsburg, New York"), where it meets [Seven Lakes Drive](/wiki/Seven_Lakes_Drive "Seven Lakes Drive") and connects to [CR 72](/wiki/County_Route_72_(Rockland_County,_New_York) "County Route 72 (Rockland County, New York)") by way of a modified trumpet interchange.[[7]](#cite_note-HarrimanNJ-7)

Outside of Sloatsburg, NY 17 becomes a four-lane [expressway](/wiki/Limited-access_road "Limited-access road") and winds its way southeasterly along the [Ramapo River](/wiki/Ramapo_River "Ramapo River") and the Thruway through the town of [Ramapo](/wiki/Ramapo,_New_York "Ramapo, New York") to the hamlet of the same name, based just north of NY 17's junction with [NY 59](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_59 "New York State Route 59"). Here, NY 17 turns to the southwest, merging onto the Thruway southbound at exit 15A, traversing a sparsely developed area of the village of [Hillburn](/wiki/Hillburn,_New_York "Hillburn, New York"). About 0.75 miles (1.21 km) south of merging onto the Thruway, NY 17 passes to the south of the village center as it approaches [I-287](/wiki/Interstate_287 "Interstate 287"). At exit 15A for [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), NY 17 leaves the Thruway and merges with I-287 southbound and proceeds to the New Jersey state line, where it connects to New Jersey's [Route 17](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_17 "New Jersey Route 17").[[7]](#cite_note-HarrimanNJ-7) An old alignment of NY 17 in Hillburn, now called Old Route 17 used to go around the Thruway instead of merging onto it.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Origins")]

#### Before the automobile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Before the automobile")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Map_of_Liberty_Highway.png/500px-Map_of_Liberty_Highway.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Liberty_Highway.png)

Map of the Liberty Highway

From Binghamton to Corning, NY 17 follows the course of the Great Bend and Bath Turnpike, which was legislated in 1808 to continue the [Cochecton and Great Bend Turnpike](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_371 "Pennsylvania Route 371") (US 11) through the Susquehanna Valley. The road ran from the Pennsylvania state line at Great Bend through Binghamton, Owego, and Elmira to Bath. In its day, it was a major route of travel through the Susquehanna Valley.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Today, the road is designated US 11 from Pennsylvania to Binghamton, then NY 17C to Waverly, NY 352 into Corning, and NY 415 to Bath. The at-grade sections of NY 17 in Orange County follow the Orange Turnpike south of Southfields and the New Windsor and Cornwall Turnpike to its north.

#### Liberty Highway and Legislative Route 4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Liberty Highway and Legislative Route 4")]

The original routing of NY 17, from [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield_(village),_New_York "Westfield (village), New York") to [Harriman](/wiki/Harriman,_New_York "Harriman, New York"), was largely designated in 1908 by the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") as Route 4, an unsigned [legislative route](/wiki/Legislative_route "Legislative route"). This routing was incorporated in 1918 as the main portion of an [auto trail](/wiki/Auto_trail "Auto trail") called the [Liberty Highway](/wiki/Liberty_Highway "Liberty Highway"), which connected [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") to [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") via [Hackensack](/wiki/Hackensack,_New_Jersey "Hackensack, New Jersey"), [Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_(town),_New_York "Liberty (town), New York"), the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"), and [Erie](/wiki/Erie "Erie").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Legislative Route 4 began at legislative Route 18 (current [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York")) in Westfield and proceeded southeast through [Mayville](/wiki/Mayville,_New_York "Mayville, New York") to [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") on what is now [NY 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394") and [NY 430](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_430 "New York State Route 430"). From there, the route headed generally eastward to [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York") over modern NY 394, [NY 242](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_242 "New York State Route 242"), and [NY 353](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_353 "New York State Route 353"), and southeast to [Olean](/wiki/Olean,_New_York "Olean, New York") via [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417"). At Olean, the route shifted northward, passing through [Hinsdale](/wiki/Hinsdale,_New_York "Hinsdale, New York"), [Friendship](/wiki/Friendship,_New_York "Friendship, New York"), and [Belvidere](/wiki/Belvidere,_New_York "Belvidere, New York") on current [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16"), [NY 446](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_446 "New York State Route 446") and Allegany County's [CR 20](/wiki/County_Route_20_(Allegany_County,_New_York) "County Route 20 (Allegany County, New York)") before returning southward on what is now [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19") to access the village of [Wellsville](/wiki/Wellsville_(village),_New_York "Wellsville (village), New York").[[12]](#cite_note-1909book-12)[[13]](#cite_note-1920book-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/SR_17_NY.jpg/250px-SR_17_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:SR_17_NY.jpg)

NY 17 at Liberty, New York

From Wellsville to [Andover](/wiki/Andover_(village),_New_York "Andover (village), New York") and from [Jasper](/wiki/Jasper,_New_York "Jasper, New York") to [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York"), Route 4 followed modern NY 417. In between Andover and Jasper, however, Route 4 veered north on current [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") and [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") to serve [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York"). East of Corning, the alignment of legislative Route 4 ran along the Great Bend and Bath Turnpike, and more closely resembled the modern alignments of the Southern Tier Expressway and the Quickway. Route 4 exited Corning on what is now [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") and followed it to [Big Flats](/wiki/Big_Flats_(CDP),_New_York "Big Flats (CDP), New York"), where it broke from NY 352 and proceeded to [Horseheads](/wiki/Horseheads_(village),_New_York "Horseheads (village), New York") on Chemung CR 64 and to [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira_(city),_New_York "Elmira (city), New York") on what is now Lake Road, Madison Avenue and the east end of [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352"). Between Elmira and [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton_(city),_New_York "Binghamton (city), New York"), Route 4 followed either local roads that were bypassed or upgraded into the Southern Tier Expressway, namely modern NY 17 and Chemung and Tioga CR 60 from Elmira to [Waverly](/wiki/Waverly,_Tioga_County,_New_York "Waverly, Tioga County, New York"), [NY 17C](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17C "New York State Route 17C") between Waverly and [Owego](/wiki/Owego_(village),_New_York "Owego (village), New York"), [NY 434](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_434 "New York State Route 434") from Owego to [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York"), and NY 17C and Riverside Drive (via [NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26")) from Vestal to Binghamton.[[12]](#cite_note-1909book-12)[[13]](#cite_note-1920book-13)

Route 4 exited the city on [US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11_in_New_York "U.S. Route 11 in New York") and followed it to Kirkwood Center, a hamlet adjacent to the eastern junction of NY 17 and [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York"). From this point to Harriman, except for one section near [Middletown](/wiki/Middletown,_Orange_County,_New_York "Middletown, Orange County, New York"), the path Route 4 followed became the basis for the Quickway several decades later. Between Kirkwood Center and Hancock, Route 4 utilized what was later upgraded into the Quickway (via Broome CR 28 from [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(town),_New_York "Windsor (town), New York") to [Deposit](/wiki/Deposit_(village),_New_York "Deposit (village), New York")). East of here, it used parallel roads instead: modern "Old Route 17" (Delaware CR 17 and Sullivan CRs 179A to 174) from Deposit to [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello,_New_York "Monticello, New York"), Sullivan CRs 173 to 171 between Monticello and [Bloomingburg](/wiki/Bloomingburg,_New_York "Bloomingburg, New York"), and Orange CR 76 and [NY 17M](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17M "New York State Route 17M") from Bloomingburg to Harriman. At Harriman, Route 4 broke from the Liberty Highway and proceeded northeast over current [US 6](/wiki/U.S._Route_6_in_New_York "U.S. Route 6 in New York") and [NY 293](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_293 "New York State Route 293") to [Highland Falls](/wiki/Highland_Falls,_New_York "Highland Falls, New York"), where it ended at legislative Route 3 (modern [US 9W](/wiki/U.S._Route_9W "U.S. Route 9W")).[[12]](#cite_note-1909book-12)[[13]](#cite_note-1920book-13) The portion of the Liberty Highway between [Suffern](/wiki/Suffern,_New_York "Suffern, New York") and Harriman became part of legislative Route 39-b in 1911;[[13]](#cite_note-1920book-13)[[14]](#cite_note-1919book-14) however, this designation was removed on March 1, 1921.[[15]](#cite_note-1921book-15) Another auto trail, the West Shore Route, also followed this section of the Liberty Highway, but proceeded north from Harimman along modern-day NY 32.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Designation and early changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Designation and early changes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/NY17RandomTrafficLight.jpg/250px-NY17RandomTrafficLight.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY17RandomTrafficLight.jpg)

The original exit 98, a signalized intersection in Parksville. A nearly 3-mile (4.8 km) bypass around the hamlet opened in 2011 to replace the junction.

When New York first signed its state highways with route numbers in 1924, much of legislative Route 4 was designated as NY 17.[[2]](#cite_note-1924nyt-2) From [Randolph](/wiki/Randolph_(village),_New_York "Randolph (village), New York") to Salamanca, NY 17 followed the more southerly routing of the Liberty Highway instead of the Route 4 routing, bypassing [Little Valley](/wiki/Little_Valley_(village),_New_York "Little Valley (village), New York") to the south in favor of a direct connection between Randolph and Salamanca (current NY 394 and [NY 951T](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_951T "New York State Route 951T")).[[17]](#cite_note-1926map1-17) In Vestal, NY 17 was routed along the south bank of the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River"), bypassing [Endicott](/wiki/Endicott,_New_York "Endicott, New York") and [Johnson City](/wiki/Johnson_City,_New_York "Johnson City, New York") on what is now NY 434 and Broome CR 44.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Lastly, NY 17 broke from the path of legislative Route 4 in Harriman and followed the former Route 39-b south to the [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") state line at Suffern.[[17]](#cite_note-1926map1-17) As originally laid out, NY 17 was 434 miles (698 km) long.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"), NY 17 basically remained intact. The only changes made at this time were the straightening out of the Olean–Wellsville segment (now via [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres,_New_York "Ceres, New York")) and the Andover–Jasper segment (now via [Greenwood](/wiki/Greenwood,_New_York "Greenwood, New York")).[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20)

NY 17 initially reached New Jersey by way of Suffern's Orange Avenue (now [US 202](/wiki/U.S._Route_202_in_New_York "U.S. Route 202 in New York")) and connected to New Jersey's [Route 2](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_2 "New Jersey Route 2") at the state line. In 1932, an alternate route of NY 17 between the New Jersey state line at [Hillburn](/wiki/Hillburn,_New_York "Hillburn, New York") and the hamlet of [Ramapo](/wiki/Ramapo,_New_York "Ramapo, New York") on the western bank of the [Ramapo River](/wiki/Ramapo_River "Ramapo River") was designated as [NY 339](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_59#Designation "New York State Route 59"). The route largely followed the path of modern [I-287](/wiki/Interstate_287 "Interstate 287") and the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") between the two locations. It initially became a local road upon crossing into New Jersey;[[21]](#cite_note-21) however, Route 2 was realigned c. 1933 to connect to NY 339 instead of NY 17.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In the mid-1930s, the alignments of NY 17 and NY 339 south of Ramapo were flipped, placing NY 17 on the western route.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 1938, NY 17 was relocated onto a new highway through the Hillburn village limits. While the southern half of the new road utilized the old highway, the northern half veered to the west of both Hillburn and old NY 17, bypassing the village before rejoining the old road south of Ramapo.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Late 20th century conversion into expressway and later improvements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Late 20th century conversion into expressway and later improvements")]

Main article: [Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)")

The explosive growth of the [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") industry in the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") region, which began in the 1930s and intensified after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), stretched the rural road to its limits. Scores of hotels, resorts and bungalow colonies attracted hundreds of thousands of vacationing New Yorkers, whose cars left the two-lane NY 17 hopelessly jammed in summer. Many towns, especially the fairly large city of [Middletown](/wiki/Middletown,_Orange_County,_New_York "Middletown, Orange County, New York"), were paralyzed on Friday evenings and Sunday afternoons in the summertime, as traffic passed through local downtowns and their traffic lights.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In addition, the tight turns and steep inclines along the route led to numerous fatal crashes, including two milk tanker truck crashes in the mid-1950s. In response, [New York State](/wiki/New_York_State "New York State") officials planned a four-lane replacement, the first free long-distance expressway in the state and one of the earliest in the United States. It would replace intersections with well-spaced access ramps, separate grades with [flyovers](/wiki/Overpass "Overpass"), and allow safe travel at up to 65 miles per hour (105 km/h).[[27]](#cite_note-1958nyt-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Old_NY_17_through_Red_House.jpg/250px-Old_NY_17_through_Red_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_NY_17_through_Red_House.jpg)

NY 17's former alignment in the town of [Red House](/wiki/Red_House,_New_York "Red House, New York"), abandoned and banned from traffic

The first segment of the new highway extended from [Fair Oaks](/wiki/Fair_Oaks,_New_York "Fair Oaks, New York") to [Goshen](/wiki/Goshen_(village),_New_York "Goshen (village), New York"), bypassing the city of Middletown to the northeast. It opened to traffic in July 1951 as a realignment of NY 17.[[27]](#cite_note-1958nyt-27)[[28]](#cite_note-1952map-28) As more sections of the freeway—known as the Quickway—opened up during the 1950s and 1960s, NY 17 was moved onto them. The Quickway was completed by 1968, connecting Binghamton to Harriman by way of a continuous expressway.[[27]](#cite_note-1958nyt-27)[[29]](#cite_note-1968map-29) Farther west, plans were also in the works to build an expressway across the Southern Tier. The highway was first proposed by New York Governor [Thomas Dewey](/wiki/Thomas_Dewey "Thomas Dewey") in 1953,[[30]](#cite_note-30) and the first sections of the Southern Tier Expressway were completed in the mid-1960s. At the time, NY 17 followed the entirety of two of the four open sections ([Steamburg](/wiki/Steamburg,_New_York "Steamburg, New York") to Salamanca and Owego to the [Broome County](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York") line) and part of a third (Corning to Lowman via Elmira).[[29]](#cite_note-1968map-29)[[31]](#cite_note-1965map-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Red_House_Bridge_August_2015.jpg/250px-Red_House_Bridge_August_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_House_Bridge_August_2015.jpg)

The bridge over the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") in Red House, due to be replaced

As more continuous pieces of the expressway opened during the 1970s, NY 17 was realigned onto them, with much of NY 17's old alignment becoming NY 394, NY 417, or NY 17C. By 1980, the expressway was complete from [Bemus Point](/wiki/Bemus_Point,_New_York "Bemus Point, New York") to Binghamton except for two areas near Salamanca and Corning. Although NY 17 continued to extend northwestward along its original alignment from Bemus Point to Westfield, both highways were also designated as parts of NY 430 and NY 394 in anticipation of the completion of the Southern Tier Expressway west of [Chautauqua Lake](/wiki/Chautauqua_Lake "Chautauqua Lake"), which NY 17 would be rerouted to follow.[[32]](#cite_note-1972map-32)[[33]](#cite_note-1980map-33) This segment was built in stages during the 1980s as a [super two](/wiki/Super_two "Super two") highway.[[34]](#cite_note-1985map-34)[[35]](#cite_note-1989map-35) The portions of the freeway in and around Salamanca and Corning were completed in the late 1980s[[34]](#cite_note-1985map-34)[[35]](#cite_note-1989map-35) and mid-1990s, completing the conversion of NY 17 into a continuous expressway from the Pennsylvania state line to Harriman.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In 1996, both exit 117 and exit 118A were closed.

The large portion of the road west of Chautauqua Lake was widened to four lanes as part of a project completed in 1997.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

On December 3, 1999, the westernmost 177 miles (284.85 km) of NY 17 were replaced by [I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)"),[[39]](#cite_note-i86map-39) a new route that had been written into law a year earlier. As legislated, I-86 will eventually extend eastward along the length of both the Southern Tier Expressway and the Quickway to the New York State Thruway in Harriman once both highways are brought up to [Interstate Highway standards](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_standards "Interstate Highway standards").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

I-86 was extended east to Horseheads in 2004,[[39]](#cite_note-i86map-39) and Elmira in 2008.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

A 10-mile (16 km) stretch of NY 17 in central [Broome County](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York") was designated as I-86 in 2006.[[39]](#cite_note-i86map-39)

In September 2013, the [Federal Highway Administration](/wiki/Federal_Highway_Administration "Federal Highway Administration") (FHWA) approved a 15.8-mile (25.4 km) extension of the I-86 designation from exit 56 in Elmira to the Tioga county line.[[42]](#cite_note-exit60-42) The remainder of NY 17 west of [I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") was designated after the remaining at-grade sections are eliminated and the highway is brought up to Interstate Highway standards.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Parksville_Panorama.jpg/960px-Parksville_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parksville_Panorama.jpg "The former alignment of NY 17 through Parksville in February 2012, after construction of the bypass around the hamlet. Downtown Parksville is visible to the right.")

The former alignment of NY 17 through Parksville in February 2012, after construction of the bypass around the hamlet. Downtown Parksville is visible to the right.

The [New York State Thruway Authority](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway_Authority "New York State Thruway Authority") converted the Harriman Toll Barrier at the interchange of NY 17 and I-87 (exit 16 on I-87) to cashless tolling. This included the creation of a solar photovoltaic energy generating facility (solar park) to help power the toll and maintenance facilities in Harriman, Woodbury, Spring Valley, and Nyack. Cashless tolling began on the night of September 27, 2018. This was a part of [Governor Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo")'s goal to convert the entirety of the New York Thruway to cashless tolling.[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/NY_17_exit_125.jpg/250px-NY_17_exit_125.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_17_exit_125.jpg)

The new exit 125 before completion

On August 9, 2019, Governor Andrew Cuomo signed into law that a portion of NY 17 was to be designated the "Dennis 'Matt' Howe Memorial Highway" from exit 63 in the hamlet of Lounsberry to exit 62 in the village of Nichols. On March 18, 2019, Howe died from injuries sustained when a tractor-trailer collided with his DOT truck as he and others were performing highway safety work on NY 17. The signs were unveiled and the dedication ceremony was held on October 29, 2019.[[46]](#cite_note-SBill-46)

In November 2019, NYSDOT completed the reconstruction of exit 131 along NY 17. As part of the project, a [diverging diamond interchange](/wiki/Diverging_diamond_interchange "Diverging diamond interchange") was built at the exit to improve access between NY 17 and NY 32. Also as part of the project, NY 32 was widened to three lanes to each way; [CR 64](/wiki/List_of_county_routes_in_Orange_County,_New_York#Routes_51_and_up "List of county routes in Orange County, New York") / Nininger Road was extended to [Woodbury Common Premium Outlets](/wiki/Woodbury_Common_Premium_Outlets "Woodbury Common Premium Outlets"); and exit ramps were built from NY 32 north and NY 32 south to meet Nininger Road at two respective traffic circles. In addition, a new park-and-ride was built, and the ramp from NY 17 west to Woodbury Common was demolished.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In December 2020, NYSDOT completed construction of a new exit 125, which was built to accommodate the new [Legoland New York](/wiki/Legoland_New_York "Legoland New York"). As part of the project, a [four-ramp parclo](/wiki/Partial_cloverleaf_interchange "Partial cloverleaf interchange") was built, which replaced the prior exit 125, located 4,000 feet (1.2 km) west. NY 17 was expanded to three lanes in each direction between exits 124 and 125. Harriman Drive was expanded to two lanes in each direction between the exit and Legoland's entrance.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

On November 14, 2024, NYSDOT announced the extension of the I-86 designation from exit 60 to exit 67 ([NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26")) in [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Future")]

### Widening in Orange and Sullivan counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Widening in Orange and Sullivan counties")]

NYSDOT has requested proposals for plans to widen NY 17 between the Thruway in Orange County and the town of Liberty in Sullivan County. The widening is supported by the 17-Forward-86 coalition, several state senators, and various businesses and organizations in the area.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

New York State Department of Transportation wants to begin a $1.3 billion project in the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") section of the route.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Suffixed routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_17&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Suffixed routes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/NY_17J_topo.PNG/250px-NY_17J_topo.PNG)](/wiki/File:NY_17J_topo.PNG)

NY 17 and NY 17J marked on a 1948 topographical map

NY 17 has had 13 suffixed routes bearing 11 different designations. Five are still assigned to their routes, while eight have been removed or renumbered. A fourteenth, [NY 17L](/wiki/NY_17L "NY 17L"), was proposed in 1939 as part of current [NY 97](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_97 "New York State Route 97"), but canceled.[[57]](#cite_note-NY_17L-4-57)

* The NY 17A designation has been used for two distinct highways:
  + The [first NY 17A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_242 "New York State Route 242") was an alternate route of NY 17 between [Randolph](/wiki/Randolph_(village),_New_York "Randolph (village), New York") and Salamanca via [Little Valley](/wiki/Little_Valley_(village),_New_York "Little Valley (village), New York"). It was assigned in the mid-1920s[[2]](#cite_note-1924nyt-2)[[17]](#cite_note-1926map1-17) and renumbered to [NY 17H](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17H_(1930%E2%80%93mid-1930s) "New York State Route 17H (1930–mid-1930s)") in the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)").[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20)
  + The [current NY 17A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17A "New York State Route 17A") (24.76 miles or 39.85 kilometres) is an alternate route of NY 17 between [Goshen](/wiki/Goshen_(village),_New_York "Goshen (village), New York") and [Southfields](/wiki/Southfields,_New_York "Southfields, New York") that connects NY 17 to [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick,_New_York "Warwick, New York") in [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York").[[1]](#cite_note-2008tvr-1) It was assigned as part of the 1930 renumbering.[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20)
* [NY 17B](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17B "New York State Route 17B") (21.90 miles or 35.24 kilometres) is a spur of NY 17 connecting [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello,_New_York "Monticello, New York") to [NY 97](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_97 "New York State Route 97") in [Callicoon](/wiki/Callicoon_(CDP),_New_York "Callicoon (CDP), New York").[[1]](#cite_note-2008tvr-1) When it was originally assigned in 1930, it also extended northward to [Hancock](/wiki/Hancock_(village),_New_York "Hancock (village), New York") via modern NY 97.[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20)
* [NY 17C](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17C "New York State Route 17C") (40.34 miles or 64.92 kilometres) is an alternate route of NY 17 between [Waverly](/wiki/Waverly,_Tioga_County,_New_York "Waverly, Tioga County, New York") and [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York") in [Tioga](/wiki/Tioga_County,_New_York "Tioga County, New York") and [Broome](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York") counties.[[1]](#cite_note-2008tvr-1) The western terminus of the route was initially located in [Owego](/wiki/Owego_(village),_New_York "Owego (village), New York") upon being assigned in 1930.[[58]](#cite_note-1930map-58)
* [NY 17D](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_427 "New York State Route 427") was a spur of NY 17 in the vicinity of [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York"), [Chemung County](/wiki/Chemung_County,_New_York "Chemung County, New York") that ran from downtown Elmira to the [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") border east of [Wellsburg](/wiki/Wellsburg,_New_York "Wellsburg, New York"). The route, assigned in 1930,[[58]](#cite_note-1930map-58) was renumbered to [NY 427](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_427 "New York State Route 427") in the early 1940s.[[59]](#cite_note-1940map-59)[[60]](#cite_note-1942map-60)
* [NY 17E](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") was an alternate route of NY 17 between [Big Flats](/wiki/Big_Flats_(CDP),_New_York "Big Flats (CDP), New York") and Elmira in Chemung County. The route, assigned in 1930,[[58]](#cite_note-1930map-58) was renumbered to [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") in October 1966 after local community pressure.[[61]](#cite_note-17Erenumbered-61)
* [NY 17F](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17F "New York State Route 17F") was an alternate route of NY 17 between [Andover](/wiki/Andover_(village),_New_York "Andover (village), New York"), [Allegany County](/wiki/Allegany_County,_New_York "Allegany County, New York"), and [Addison](/wiki/Addison_(village),_New_York "Addison (village), New York"), [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York"). The route, assigned in 1930, was a northerly alternate route of NY 17 that served [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") and [Canisteo](/wiki/Canisteo_(village),_New_York "Canisteo (village), New York").[[62]](#cite_note-62) NY 17F was removed in the early 1940s and is now [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21"), [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") and Steuben County's [CR 119](/wiki/County_Route_119_(Steuben_County,_New_York) "County Route 119 (Steuben County, New York)").[[59]](#cite_note-1940map-59)[[60]](#cite_note-1942map-60)
* [NY 17G](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_248 "New York State Route 248") was a spur assigned in 1930 to what is now [NY 248](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_248 "New York State Route 248") south of NY 417.[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20) It became part of an extended NY 248 in the early 1940s.[[59]](#cite_note-1940map-59)[[60]](#cite_note-1942map-60)
* The NY 17H designation has been used for two distinct highways:
  + The [first NY 17H](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_242 "New York State Route 242") was an alternate route of NY 17 between Randolph and Salamanca via Little Valley in [Cattaraugus County](/wiki/Cattaraugus_County,_New_York "Cattaraugus County, New York"). It was assigned in 1930[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20) and removed c. 1937. The route became part of an extended [NY 242](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_242 "New York State Route 242") west of Little Valley and remained part of [NY 18](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_18 "New York State Route 18") (which NY 17H overlapped) east of the village (that portion of NY 18 became [NY 353](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_353 "New York State Route 353") in 1962).[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)
  + The [second NY 17H](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_201 "New York State Route 201") was a spur route of NY 17 in the vicinity of [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton_(city),_New_York "Binghamton (city), New York"). The route began at NY 17 in Binghamton and followed Riverside Drive west and north to [Johnson City](/wiki/Johnson_City,_New_York "Johnson City, New York"), where it ended at NY 17C. It was assigned by 1940[[59]](#cite_note-1940map-59)[[65]](#cite_note-65) and partially replaced with [NY 201](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_201 "New York State Route 201") in September 1971.[[66]](#cite_note-redesignated-66)
* [NY 17J](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394") was a westerly alternate route of NY 17 between [Mayville](/wiki/Mayville,_New_York "Mayville, New York") and [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") along the western edge of [Chautauqua Lake](/wiki/Chautauqua_Lake "Chautauqua Lake"). It was assigned in 1930[[20]](#cite_note-1930nyt-20) and supplanted by [NY 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394") in November 1973.[[67]](#cite_note-ny17Jconversion-67)
* [NY 17K](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17K "New York State Route 17K") (22.37 miles or 36.00 kilometres) is a spur linking NY 17 in [Bloomingburg](/wiki/Bloomingburg,_New_York "Bloomingburg, New York") to [US 9W](/wiki/U.S._Route_9W "U.S. Route 9W") and [NY 32](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_32 "New York State Route 32") in [Newburgh](/wiki/Newburgh,_New_York "Newburgh, New York"), Orange County.[[1]](#cite_note-2008tvr-1) It was assigned c. 1939.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)
* [NY 17M](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17M "New York State Route 17M") (26.63 miles or 42.86 kilometres) is the former routing of NY 17 in Orange County. The route begins north of Middletown in [Fair Oaks](/wiki/Fair_Oaks,_New_York "Fair Oaks, New York") and rejoins NY 17 in [Harriman](/wiki/Harriman,_New_York "Harriman, New York").[[1]](#cite_note-2008tvr-1) It was assigned in 1951.[[27]](#cite_note-1958nyt-27)[[28]](#cite_note-1952map-28)
